explanation: The phrase 'initialization failure' maps to the atomic proposition initFail. The requirement 'the user shall be warned that the system is out-of-service' translates to the atomic proposition OutOfServiceWarning. The phrase 'any other operations shall be not allowed' is interpreted as the system not being in its primary operating state, which is 'ventilating', so it translates to !ventilating. The two consequences, 'OutOfServiceWarning' and '!ventilating', are connected by 'In addition', which means they both must be true, forming OutOfServiceWarning & !ventilating. The overall structure 'In case of X, Y. In addition Z.' implies a global implication where if X happens, then Y and Z must always hold. This translates to a global implication G(initFail -> (OutOfServiceWarning & !ventilating)).

explanation: The phrase 'In case of a (initialization) failure' directly maps to the atomic proposition 'initFail'. The first consequence, 'the user shall be warned that the system is out-of-service', implies that 'OutOfServiceWarning' will eventually (F) become true. The second consequence, 'any other operations shall be not allowed', indicates that the system must enter a state where normal operations are prohibited. Given that 'initFail' is a severe condition, the system entering 'failSafeMode' (which describes a state where normal operations are suspended, with specific valve positions 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen') is the appropriate representation. This mode should persist indefinitely from the point it is entered, indicated by 'G failSafeMode'. The overall formula then expresses that if 'initFail' occurs, it eventually leads to a state where both 'OutOfServiceWarning' is true, and from that point, 'failSafeMode' is continuously maintained.

explanation: The phrase 'In case of a (initialization) failure' defines the condition, which maps to the atomic proposition 'initFail'. Since this is a general rule that applies at any time, we use the global operator G, resulting in 'G(initFail -> ...)'. The first consequence is 'the user shall be warned that the system is out-of-service'. 'Shall be warned' implies that this state will eventually occur, so we use the F (finally) operator with 'OutOfServiceWarning', giving 'F OutOfServiceWarning'. The second consequence, 'any other operations shall be not allowed', means that from the point of the failure onwards, the system should not engage in its primary operational modes. We interpret 'any other operations' as the main ventilation modes: 'PSVMode', 'PCVMode', and the general 'ventilating' state. 'Shall be not allowed' means these conditions must always be false from that point in time, which translates to 'G(!(PSVMode | PCVMode | ventilating))'. The phrase 'In addition' connects these two consequences with a logical AND (&). Combining these, the complete formula is G(initFail -> (F OutOfServiceWarning & G(!(PSVMode | PCVMode | ventilating)))).

explanation: The phrase 'initialization failure' directly maps to the atomic proposition 'initFail'. The phrase 'the system is out-of-service' maps to 'OutOfServiceWarning'. The first part of the sentence 'In case of a (initialization) failure the user shall be warned that the system is out-of-service' implies that globally, if an initialization failure occurs, the system must issue an out-of-service warning. This translates to G(initFail -> OutOfServiceWarning). For 'any other operations shall be not allowed', 'any other operations' refers to active ventilation modes or procedures. Based on the provided atomic propositions, this includes 'ventilating', 'PCVMode', 'PSVMode', 'RM', 'inspiratoryPause', and 'expiratoryPause'. So, 'any other operations' can be represented as (ventilating | PCVMode | PSVMode | RM | inspiratoryPause | expiratoryPause). 'Shall be not allowed' means these operations must not be true, so !(ventilating | PCVMode | PSVMode | RM | inspiratoryPause | expiratoryPause). The 'In addition' indicates that this condition also holds true under 'initFail'. Combining both parts, if 'initFail' is true, then both 'OutOfServiceWarning' and the negation of any other operations must be true. The 'In case of' implies a global property, so the entire implication is under a G operator.

explanation:
The phrase "In case of a (initialization) failure" translates to the atomic proposition 'initFail'. The overall structure "In case of X, Y. In addition, Z." implies a global implication: G(X -> (Y & Z)).
The first consequence, "the user shall be warned that the system is out-of-service," translates to 'F OutOfServiceWarning', as 'shall be warned' implies that the warning will eventually occur.
The phrase "In addition" signifies a conjunction '&' between the consequences.
The second consequence, "any other operations shall be not allowed," indicates that the system must enter a state where normal operations are ceased and remain so. The atomic proposition 'failSafeMode' is defined as a state where the machine is brought to a fail-safe mode (in valve closed and out valve open), which perfectly represents operations being disallowed. To ensure this state is entered and persists, 'F G failSafeMode' is used, meaning eventually the system enters and stays in fail-safe mode. Therefore, the complete formula is G(initFail -> (F OutOfServiceWarning & F G failSafeMode)).

explanation: The phrase "In case of a (initialization) failure" translates to the atomic proposition "initFail". The 'In case of' indicates a global property, meaning that if this condition occurs at any point, the specified consequences must follow, represented by the G (globally) operator over an implication (->). The first consequence, "the user shall be warned that the system is out-of-service", means that the state "OutOfServiceWarning" must eventually (F) become true. The second consequence, "any other operations shall be not allowed", implies that the system must enter a state where normal operations are prevented. The atomic proposition "failSafeMode" is defined as the machine being brought to a state (in valve closed and out valve open) where normal operations are not allowed. explanation: The phrase 'In case of a (initialization) failure' directly maps to the atomic proposition 'initFail'. The phrase 'the user shall be warned that the system is out-of-service' maps to the atomic proposition 'OutOfServiceWarning'. The structure 'In case of A, B' implies an implication, A -> B. 'In addition' signifies a conjunction (&) with the subsequent condition. 'any other operations shall be not allowed' is interpreted as a set of key normal operational states and activities being false. This includes not ventilating (!ventilating), not being in active ventilation modes (!PCVMode & !PSVMode), not allowing ventilation parameters to be adjustable (!ventilationParamsAdjustable), not performing specific ventilation procedures (!RM & !expiratoryPause & !inspiratoryPause), not starting general health monitoring/reporting (!startMonitoring & !startReportingHealthParams), and not saving normal operational logs (!saveLog). Finally, 'Globally' applies the G (always) operator to the entire formula, meaning this condition must hold at all times.
